name: Anonymous
comment:
https://youtu.be/rAEtxySLBoo
Imagine you spent 40 years doing the boring, responsible thing.

You opened a 401k at 23. You contributed every paycheck. You ignored the noise. You bought the index because Bogle told you to, because Buffett told you to, because every honest piece of financial advice for 30 years told you the index was the safest, most diversified, most rules-based way to own America.

The whole point was the rules.

The rules said: a company must trade for 12 months before joining the S&P 500. The rules said: it must show four consecutive quarters of GAAP profitability. The rules existed because in 1999 the index quietly bought a lot of stocks at the top, and pensioners paid the bill.

After the dot-com crash, S&P tightened the rules. Nasdaq tightened the rules. FTSE Russell tightened the rules.

For 23 years, those rules held.

Then SpaceX filed for IPO.

And the rules changed.

The S&P 500 waived the profitability requirement. Nasdaq cut its trading-history window from 90 days to 15. FTSE Russell cut its to 5.

Bloomberg Intelligence estimates the major index funds will absorb between 19% and 24% of SpaceX's float within six months. That's over $30 trillion of passive 401k and retirement money, mechanically buying a single newly public company at IPO valuations, because the rules said they had to.

Except the rules used to say they didn't.

Here's the thought exercise:

If you spend 40 years building a system designed to protect ordinary savers from buying overpriced stocks, and then you waive the protections the moment a sufficiently large stock asks you to, what was the system actually protecting?

Most of investing is about understanding what's a rule and what's a guideline.

A rule binds the rule-maker.

A guideline binds the saver.

You're allowed to find out which is which only after the fact.

Maybe not in our lifetime. Maybe not even in our children's. But every generation inherits the consequences of what the previous one believed was possible.

If everyone shrugs and says "it will never happen," then it truly never will. Every developed region today was once poor, unstable, corrupt, divided, or all of the above. Change takes decades, sometimes centuries.

I don't have blind faith that we'll wake up one day and see a transformed Latin America. What I do believe is that if we don't start building toward that future now, then when exactly are we supposed to start? Another generation from now? Another hundred years?

Someone has to plant the tree even if they know they'll never sit in its shade.

comment:
Having grown up with the Internet and now being in my 30's, I really resent how a specific type of perpetually adolescent "normie" has completely flooded the Internet. They act like this is a giant middle school lunch room (even though they're 18, 19, 25...) and as though we're all on candid camera 24/7 instead of having semi-pseudonymous info exchanges. In most corners of the modern 'net, you'll get people acting like you're supposed to be adhering to IRL social norms and even enforcing them by doing shady shit like recording surreptitiously and doxxing to an insane degree if you don't conform to the monoculture.

When I was a kid, the Internet was a sort of wild west, Libertarian thing, with a mix of users of all ages. It was normal and expected that a board like /g/ or an IRC tech channel would have a mix of almost exclusively men aged anywhere between maybe 12 and 60, and it was almost embarrassing for someone on the younger end to admit that they were actually a child (even though it would sometimes be obvious). People wanted to engage in discussion and even have their perspectives be taken seriously on occasion, and trolling consisted of intentional logical fallacies, having really bad but elaborate "takes", etc. Now, the Web is just flooded with the exact kind of moron who is happy to be fully programmed by the monoculture, which is clearly top-down, even online, and "trolling" essentially amounts to group stalking and harassment.

Nothing is organic anymore, because the monoculture is essentially now fully derived from 'influencers' and engagement bait accounts which necessarily conform to the advertiser- and child-friendly (anti-)privacy and content policies of the big platforms, which means that the same Jews who told boomers what to think on TV are telling zoomers what to think online, and zoomers lap it up and even actively "cross the street" to ridicule those who don't participate. I am sick of these NPC golems subverting every corner of the Internet.

name: Anonymous
comment:
>>536398299
>I really don't know what people use more than 16gb for.
Well real world example, I sometimes fuck around on VRChat, and let's say I'm chilling on VR Chat, but I'm also checking email, watching videos, etc, in the background RAM allocation pushes into the high 20s.
Systems are also strong enough now that you can do something like run the desktop version of vrchat in the background and you can use it as a vtuber avatar, it can actually animate your face and hands using webcam input. So if you want to be some tranime streamer that's the easiest way to accomplish that. 16 gigs of RAM is fine for running most games. But running 2 games at once... you're going to want more. Plus a lot of people like to run playback programs so that they can clip instant replays of interesting moments in their games like if they just had a lucky knife throw or corner banged someone real good etc.
These days my minimum recommendation will always be 32 gigs for those above reasons.
Because there's ways to go well beyond just having a glorified overpriced game console and it's when you get into power user territory that suddenly the minimum minimum recommendation is no longer enough.
The "good enough" PC gaming experience stops being "good enough" quite fast.

Slings are a pretty good option for political violence. Any bans on them can only ever be bans on possession because there is no way to disrupt the supply chain of cord and rocks. But a sling is a way of escalating to lethal force (or meeting such an escalation) without spooking people as much as shooting someone.
